segments.s772-g137: دگر گفت places an adverb before the past-tense verb: دگر (here = 'again/then') + گفت ('said' — past). کای is a vocative particle ('O'), followed by شهریار جوان, the noun phrase 'young king' being addressed; word order is vocative marker + title + epithet.
segments.s772-g138: بخفتی is the archaic/past second-person singular of خوابیدن ('you slept'). The conjunction و links a contrasting clause. بیدار بودت is a stative construction where the predicate بیدار ('awake') comes before the copula بودت ('you were' — archaic/enclitic form), and روان functions as the subject/predicate noun 'spirit/soul'.
segments.s772-g139: لبت خامش is a simple nominal predicate: لبت ('your lip/your speech') + خامش ('silent'). The conjunction و begins the next phrase: جان به چندین گله uses به + noun to mark direction/manner ('into/with'), so 'the soul toward/into many complaints' — i.e., the soul departs amid many laments.
segments.s772-g140: برفت is an older past form meaning 'went away' (preverb + رفت); here it is the verb of departure. و links to the following clause: 'تنت ماند ایدر یله' has the subject تنت ('your body') + verb ماند ('remained'), with locative ایدر ('here') and predicate adjective یله ('loose/slack') following — a typical Old Persian poetic order (S V locative predicate).
segments.s772-g141: تو بیکاری is a subject + nominal predicate: 'you are idle' (bīkāri = idleness). و links the clause that follows. جان به کار اندر است is a locative construction: جان ('the soul') + به کار اندر ('in/at work') + است (copula 'is'), using the literary/archaic اندر 'in'.
segments.s772-g142: تن بدسگالت is 'your ill‑omened body' (noun + adjectival epithet). به بار اندر است is a prepositional/locative phrase 'is in/under a load' (به + بار 'burden' + اندر + است), again using the archaic اندر + copula to mean 'is burdened.'
segments.s772-g143: بگوید is the narrative/present-tense 'he says' (used here to report speech). روان‌گر is a classical conditional formation with the enclitic -گر attached to روان to create a protasis 'if the spirit...' (an older poetic device). زبان‌بسته شد is a past/result clause 'the tongue became bound/closed' (زبان‌بسته = 'tight-lipped/silent', شد = 'became').
segments.s772-g144: بیاسود is the apodosis meaning 'will rest/be at peace' (placed here before the following conditional clause, a typical poetic inversion). جان‌گر again marks the conditional 'if (the) life/spirit...' and is followed by تنت خسته شد ('your body became tired' — تنت subject, خسته adjective, شد past copula). The structure is apodosis + conditional protasis.
segments.s772-g17: بشد is a past-tense/preterite verb meaning "became" and here appears before its subject (a poetic verb-subject order). آسیابان is the (poetic) subject "the miller." دو دیده is a numeral + noun "two eyes," and پرآب is a compound adjective (پر + آب) placed after the noun to describe it: "full of water" / "tear-filled."
segments.s772-g18: به introduces a state or manner here ("in/with"), so به زردی = "in the yellowness / with the yellowness." زردی is the noun "yellowness / sallowness." دو رخساره is "two cheeks/faces" (numeric + noun), and چون + آفتاب is a simile "like the sun." Together this phrase describes the cheeks’ colour/state.
segments.s772-g19: همی + گفت expresses a continuous or iterative saying ("kept saying/was saying"); همی marks ongoing action. کای is a vocative particle "O," addressing روشن کردگار; روشن is an attributive adjective "bright/clear," and کردگار is the vocative noun "Creator/God."
segments.s772-g20: تویی is the emphatic 2nd-person present copula "thou art." برتر از is a comparative construction: برتر = "superior/above," followed by the preposition از "than/of." گردش روزگار is a compound noun phrase meaning "the turning/circuit of time" (i.e. the course or vicissitudes of time/fate).
segments.s772-g21: تو is the vocative/subject "you." زین here functions as a demonstrative/comparative marker meaning roughly "by/for/from this" (classical usage corresponds to modern از این); it links to the following noun phrase. ناپسندیده is an adjectival past-participle "unacceptable/unseemly" modifying فرمان ("command/order"), and او is the possessive pronoun "his." The whole segment frames: "you — by this unseemly command of his."
segments.s772-g22: هم‌اکنون is an adverb "right now / immediately." بپیچان is an imperative/optative form (b- prefix + پیچان) meaning "turn/twist away" or "remove" (a command form addressed to the addressee). تن و جان او is a coordinated object "his body and his life/soul," where او is the pronoun marking possession.
segments.s772-g23: بر شاه شد is a reversed order: بر + شاه + شد = "fell upon the king / the king experienced" (literally "became upon the king"). دل is the noun "heart," and پر از is the adjectival phrase "full of" (پر + از + noun). شرم و باک are the coordinated nouns "shame and dread/fear." The verb شد indicates a change of state: "the heart became full."
segments.s772-g24: رخانش is a noun with the 3rd-person possessive suffix "-ش" = "his رخان" (here referring to the face/cheeks/visage); پرآب again is the adjective "wet / full of water." و links the clauses, دهانش "his mouth" (دهان + -ش), چو is the simile particle "like/as," and خاک is the simile noun "earth/dust" (here implying dryness or ashen state).
segments.s772-g97: Word order is verb-final classical Persian: کشیدند (past plural 'they drew/pulled') governs زان آبگیر (that pond/reservoir) with the directional/locative phrase به خشکی ('onto dry ground'). زان is an archaic demonstrative ('that' / 'from that') pointing to the previously mentioned pool; here it functions as 'that' modifying آبگیر.
segments.s772-g98: بسی here works as an adverbial intensifier meaning 'much' / 'greatly' (classical usage). مویه 'lament' is a noun; کردند is the past plural verb 'they made/did' (they performed lamenting). برنا و پیر list two groups ('the young and the old'); conjunction و simply links them.
segments.s772-g99: This is a simple past clause: ساختند 'they built'. The prepositional phrase به باغ اندرون places the action 'in the garden's interior' (اندرون = 'inner/within'). دخمه‌ای is indefinite ('a tomb' — the -ای ending marks indefiniteness).
segments.s772-g100: سرش را is the direct object: سر + enclitic pronoun ش ('his') plus the object marker را. The verb افراختند is past plural 'they raised/erected'. به ابر اندر gives the direction/goal 'toward the cloud(s)' (به … اندر = 'toward/into the cloud[s]').
segments.s772-g101: سر زخم آن is a genitival phrase 'the head (tip) of that wound' (آن = 'that'). دشنه کردند is literally 'they (with a) dagger did' — here it functions as the main past verb complex meaning they dealt with or lanced/cauterized (classical/poetic usage linking a noun instrument to the action). خشک follows as a predicative/adverbial modifier meaning 'dry' (they made it dry).
segments.s772-g102: This is a list of substances applied, introduced by repeated به which here means 'with' / 'by means of'. دبق 'adhesive/resin', قیر 'tar', کافور 'camphor', مشک 'musk' are enumerated with و 'and' between them; the whole phrase modifies how they made the wound dry.
segments.s772-g103: بیاراستندش is the past plural verb بی‌آراستن 'they adorned/arranged', with the clitic ش attached to the verb meaning 'him'. به دیبای زرد is the phrase 'in/with yellow brocade' (دیبا = brocade, زرد = yellow/pale); به introduces the material/garment.
segments.s772-g104: This short noun phrase lists accoutrements: قصب زیر = 'a reed (قصب) beneath' (زیر = 'under/beneath'), و links the two items, دستی زبر لاژورد = 'a (hand-/haft-)piece (دستی) rough (زبر) of lapis-lazuli (لاژورد)'. The nouns are juxtaposed to describe decorative fittings; زبر qualifies the texture/quality of the lapis.
segments.s772-g9: This is a temporal prepositional clause: ز (from) + درگاه (the court/threshold) + ماهوی (an epithet/name, here marking whose court) gives the origin, and چون شد برون (‘when he became/out forth’) is a classical poetic phrasing meaning “when he went out.” Note the order: the origin phrase precedes the temporal clause; شد is simple past used here to mark the event of coming out.
segments.s772-g10: Two coordinated verbless state-phrases describing his condition after coming out: دو دیده پر از آب (‘two eyes full of water’) and دل پر ز خون (‘heart full of blood’). Persian often omits a copula here; پر از / پر ز patterns both mean “filled with.” The second clause uses the poetic z (ز) instead of از but functions the same.
segments.s772-g11: Standard SOV/PV order with object before verb: سواران (horsemen) is the object, فرستاد is the past-tense verb ‘sent,’ ماهوی is the agent/name (Maahvi), and زود is an adverb ‘quickly/soon’ modifying the verb. The entire phrase reads ‘Maahvi soon/quickly sent horsemen.’
segments.s772-g12: This is an elliptic nominal phrase anticipating the next line: پس (‘then’) + آسیابان (‘the miller’) + به کردار دود (‘in the manner of smoke’). There is no finite verb here; the verb appears in the following sentence. The phrase therefore sets up that the miller behaved or moved like smoke.
segments.s772-g13: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb ‘he ordered/said.’ تا introduces the content clause (‘that…’) and takes the direct objects تاج و آن گوشوار (‘the crown and that earring’) as what is to be brought or provided. Word order places the verb before the subordinate clause marker + objects.
segments.s772-g14: A continuation/clarification of the ordered items: همان (‘that same’) qualifies مهر, then و آن جامه شاه‌وار adds ‘and that kingly garment.’ These are noun phrases in apposition to the objects introduced previously; شاه‌وار is a compound adjective meaning ‘regal’ modifying جامه.
segments.s772-g15: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ‘must not / should not.’ که introduces the subordinate clause. یک‌سر (‘entirely/at once’) modifies the result, and پر از خون کنند is a verb phrase ‘make (it) full of blood’ — کنند is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person plural used after نباید to express prohibition.
segments.s772-g16: A coordinated prohibitive clause: ز تن (‘from the body’) + جامه شاه (‘the king’s garment’) + بیرون کنند (‘they pull out / remove’). Object precedes the verb as is common; کننــد is the same subjunctive verb tied to the initial نباید, so both actions are negated together.
segments.s772-g73: برفتند is the past-tense 3rd-person-plural verb 'they departed/went away'. زان is the prepositional/demonstrative compound 'from that / from those', and سوگواران is the plural noun 'mourners' (so together زان سوگواران = 'from those mourners'). بسی here qualifies quantity ('many' / 'much') placed after the noun phrase, so the whole segment reads 'many of those mourners departed.'
segments.s772-g74: This is a nominal list: سکوبا و رهبان = 'cupbearers and monks' (two sorts of persons). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and هر در کسی is a common formula meaning 'one person at every door/gate' (هر = 'each/every', در = 'door/gate', کسی = 'someone/one person'). Together: 'cupbearers and monks — someone at every door.'
segments.s772-g75: خروشی is a nominalized noun 'a cry / a commotion'. بر آمد is the compound verb 'arose / came up' (بر as prefix + آمد 'came'), so خروشی بر آمد = 'a cry arose'. ز راهب identifies the source ('from the monk'), and به درد qualifies the condition/manner as 'in sorrow / with grief' (به + درد).
segments.s772-g76: که‌ای here is the exclamatory/vocative particle (classical form of که + ای = 'O'), introducing a direct address. The following nouns — تاجور, شاه, آزادمرد — are stacked vocatives: 'O trader (tâjūr), O king, O noble/generous man.' The sequence is emphatic and vocative rather than a grammatical subject–predicate clause.
segments.s772-g77: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here rendering as 'thus said the monk'). راهب is the speaker 'the monk'. که introduces the content clause that follows. این کس ندید is an existential negative construction: این 'this', کس with ندید ('did not see') yields 'no one has seen this' (کس under negation = 'no one').
segments.s772-g78: نه ... شنید = 'nor ... heard' (نه is the negating 'not / nor'). پیش از مسیح is a temporal phrase 'before Christ' (پیش 'before', از as the linking preposition, مسیح 'Christ/Jesus'). این سخن کس شنید places این سخن 'this saying/speech' as object and کس شنید under negation means 'no one heard it' — overall 'nor before Christ has anyone heard this saying.'
segments.s772-g79: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. بر شهریاری is a circumstantial phrase 'in/over the kingship' (بر + شهریاری = 'in the exercise/holding of royal power'). زند here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning to place/keep/install (third-person); it takes بنده‌ای as its object: 'he keeps/sets a slave.' بنده‌ای is indefinite with -ای ('a/one slave').
segments.s772-g80: یکی functions as an indefinite 'one / a certain' modifying the following descriptors. بدنژادی is an adjectival compound 'low-born / of ignoble birth' and افگنده‌ای is a predicative adjective 'cast down / humbled / afflicted' with the suffix -ای echoing indefiniteness — together describing that single slave as 'a certain low-born, humbled man.'
segments.s772-g121: نه ... نه introduces a negative list: 'neither ... nor'. مهر here means 'affection, love' and is the first item; و simply links مهر to the following noun. پرستنده is a noun meaning 'devotee/attendant' (one who praises/worships) and بارگاه 'the royal court/palace' follows as the thing to which they are attached. Word order (noun + modifier) is compact and typical of epic Persian.
segments.s772-g122: A continuation of the negative list: each noun is simply enumerated with نه. افسر here means 'helmet' (not 'officer') and کشور 'realm/kingdom'. تاج 'crown' and کلاه 'cap/hat' are paired at the end; و is the coordinating 'and'. The sequence is a flat, repetitive catalogue typical of the poetic style.
segments.s772-g123: دگر گفت is a formulaic phrase meaning 'he said again' (دگر = 'again/then'). کز is the conjunction 'that / because', introducing the reason clause. خوب‌گفتار اوی is a genitive phrase: خوب‌گفتار 'eloquent / well-spoken' + اوی, a third-person possessive clitic meaning 'his', so 'because of his eloquence' (note the possessive follows the adjective/noun in this poetic word order).
segments.s772-g124: ستایش ندارم literally 'I have no praise' (ندارم = present negative 'I do not have'). سزاوار اوی is a predicate/adjectival phrase meaning 'fit/deserving of him' (سزاوار = 'deserving/appropriate', اوی = 'his'), so together 'I have no praise appropriate for him.' Note the verbless adjectival predicate follows the nominal phrase.
segments.s772-g125: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in epic Persian (here 'indeed/just' or marking the action), followed by the verb کشت 'planted' (from کشتن in the sense 'to cultivate/plant'). سرو 'cypress' is the direct object and او 'he' is the agent (poetic inversion places the subject after the verb). به باغ بهشت = 'into/in the garden of Paradise' (به + place).
segments.s772-g126: ببیند is a 3rd-person present/optative form 'he sees' or 'he beholds' (classical narrative present). روانش 'his soul' is the subject with the possessive clitic -ش. درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree', followed by the relative clause که کشت 'that he planted' (که = 'that/which', and کشت is the past 'planted' here). Word order (verb–subject–object in the previous line and verb–subject–relative here) is typical of Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s772-g127: دگر گفت 'he said again' (дگر = 'again'). یزدان is the divine name 'God' and is the implied subject of the wished action. روانت 'your soul' is object marked by the possessive -ت. ببرد is an optative/subjunctive 3rd-person form 'may (he) take/away' (برد = 'take/carry away'), so the clause expresses a divine wish: 'May God take your soul.'
segments.s772-g128: تنت را is 'your body' (تن + possessive -ت) with the definite/object marker را. بدین سوگواران = 'to these mourners' (بدین = به + این 'to these'; in the manuscript this appears fused), and سپرد is the past 'entrusted/handed over'. The whole phrase means 'he handed over (or entrusted) your body to these mourners.' Note: the tokenizer split بدین into ب and دین, but دین as given in the lemma list does not match the intended demonstrative این; therefore that token is treated below as unmatched.
segments.s772-g129: روان تو را: a topical/accusative phrase meaning “your soul,” where روان (soul) is marked by the object/definiteness marker را and follows the possessor pronoun تو (poetic order: noun + possessor). سودمند این بود: سودمند is a predicate adjective (‘beneficial’) placed before the demonstrative subject این and the copula بود (‘was’); the phrase literalmente reads “This was beneficial to your soul.” The word order (predicate before ‘این بود’) is a poetic/inverted order.
segments.s772-g130: تن بدکنش را: ‘the body of the ill‑doer’ — poetic possession without an ezāfe, where تن (body) is followed by its possessor بدکنش and then receives the object marker را. گزند این بود: گزند (‘harm’) is predicative with the demonstrative این + copula بود, so the whole means “This was the harm to the ill‑doer’s body.” Note the parallel structure to the first half: predicate phrase with این بود following the marked object.
segments.s772-g131: کنون در بهشت است بازار شاه: کنون (‘now’) opens the line; در بهشت is a locative prepositional phrase (‘in paradise’). The verb است (‘is’) appears before the subject بازار شاه (‘the king’s market’), a poetic inversion where the predicate (locative + copula) precedes the noun phrase functioning as logical subject/possessive construction (بازار with شاه as its possessor).
segments.s772-g132: به دوزخ کند جان بدخواه راه: به دوزخ (‘to/into hell’) is the goal phrase; کند is the classical/presentive form of کردن here meaning ‘causes/sends’ (timeless or habitual narration rather than modern present). جانِ بدخواه (‘the ill‑wisher’s soul’) is the object (possessive relation signaled by noun order), and راه (‘the way/road’) completes the idea: ‘sets on the road to hell’ → idiomatically ‘sends the soul to hell.’ Word order is V — object — complement, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s772-g133: دگر گفت کای شاه دانش‌پذیر: دگر here works adverbially as ‘again’ (he said again). گفت is the speech verb (‘said’) — the preparatory verb for the quoted/vocative phrase. کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ addressing شاه (king); دانش‌پذیر follows as an epithet modifying the vocative (‘receptive of knowledge’). The whole segment is a vocative/speech formula with classical word order.
segments.s772-g134: که با شهریاری و با اردشیر: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (‘who/that’) modifying the vocative king. با شهریاری (‘with/by kingship’ — accompaniment or means) and و با اردشیر (‘and with Ardashir’) are two coordinated prepositional phrases that continue the subordinate clause; the clause is left unfinished here and continues in the next lines. Note با marks accompaniment or instrumentality.
segments.s772-g135: درودی همان بر که کشتی به باغ: درودی (‘a greeting/blessing’) is qualified by همان (‘that same’) and followed by بر which here links into a relative clause introduced by که (‘which/that’). کشتی به باغ (‘the ship to the garden’) supplies the topic/subject of the relative clause (کشتی as the moving subject with به باغ indicating destination). The verb of the relative clause is postponed to the next segment (an instance of poetic enjambment): the relative clause begun by که is completed in the following words.
segments.s772-g136: درفشان شد آن خسروانی چراغ: درفشان here is used adjectivally/participialy ‘shining, glittering’ (not the bearer-of-standard sense) and شد is the past copula/verb ‘became’ — ‘became shining.’ The subject آن خسروانی چراغ (‘that royal lamp’) follows the verb (inverted order typical of epic diction). This completes the relative clause introduced by که in the previous segment: ‘which, when the ship entered the garden, that royal lamp became shining.’
segments.s772-g105: A simple coordinated list of items linked by و (and). می‌و here names the drink/fruit product (wine/grape‑product) placed among other scented substances; each و is the coordinating conjunction. چندی functions as a quantifier modifying گلاب (‘some rose‑water’).
segments.s772-g106: سکوبا is an obscure/rare noun here naming the receptacle/thing involved; بیندود is a past/archaic verbal form (3rd‑person) governing the preceding list (i.e. ‘placed/put/poured’ in older diction). بر جای خواب is an adverbial prepositional phrase ‘upon the place of sleep’ = ‘on the bed’, specifying location.
segments.s772-g107: Initial چه is the interrogative ‘what’. گفت is the main verb in past tense ‘said’ (here used as the reported‑speech verb). آن گرانمایه دهقان مرو is a definite NP ‘that noble/esteemed farmer of Merv’, with گرانمایه qualifying دهقان and مرو functioning as an epithet/locative.
segments.s772-g108: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause referring back to the farmer. بنهفت is a place or tribal name (proper noun). بالای is a prepositional/relational expression ‘on/above’, and آن زادسرو is a nominal phrase ‘that zād‑sarv’ (literally ‘cypress‑born’), here used as a place‑name or epithet.
segments.s772-g109: که introduces the clause ‘that...’. بخشش is the noun ‘giving/generosity’ or ‘donation’; ز کوشش is an ablative/causal phrase ‘from/by effort’ (z = poetic form of از), بود is a past copula ‘was/would be’, and در نهان is an adverbial phrase ‘in secret’. Together they state that giving should be the product of effort and be done secretly.
segments.s772-g110: A second clause introduced by که with a purpose/optative sense: خشنود is the predicate adjective ‘content/pleased’, بیرون is an adverb ‘out/away’, شود is a present/subjunctive or optative form ‘may become/leave’, and زین جهان (classical compressed form where ز = از + این) means ‘from this world’ = ‘from this life’. The clause expresses the aim/result: ‘that he may leave this world content.’
segments.s772-g111: دگر functions adverbially ‘again’ or ‘further’; گفت is the past reporting verb ‘said’; اگر here introduces a concessive/conditional clause ‘if/even if’; چند is used adverbially as ‘a little/somewhat’ (classical usage often = ‘though’ or ‘somewhat’); خندان بود is the predicate ‘was smiling’ (خندان adjective + past copula).
segments.s772-g112: چنان دان is an instruction/imperative ‘consider it so’ (دان = consider/think). که introduces the content clause ‘that...’; از دردمندان بود is a predication ‘he was of the sorrowful / among the afflicted’ (از = from/of, دردمندان = those in pain/sorrowful, بود = past copula). The whole gives the intended judgment: treat him as one of the afflicted.
segments.s772-g113: که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'who/that'. از + چرخ گردان is a possessive/agentive phrase: چرخ here refers to the turning Wheel/heaven/fate and گردان is an adjective 'turning/revolving'. پذیرد is a 3rd-person present form 'accepts' (classical present/subjunctive form) and takes فریب as its object meaning 'deception/trick'. The clause literally: 'that accepts deception from the turning Wheel.'
segments.s772-g114: که again introduces a relative clause modifying the same antecedent ('which/that'). او را is the 3rd-person pronoun with the definite-object/topic marker را. نماید is a 3rd-person present form of 'to show' (classical present/subjunctive/indicative: 'shows' or 'may show'). فراز و نشیب is a fixed pair meaning 'ups and downs' (fraz = high point, neshīb = low point). The clause: 'which shows him highs and lows.'
segments.s772-g115: دگر گفت is a formulaic opening 'another said' or 'he further said' (دگر = another/again; گفت here a speech verb). کآن را (archaic) functions as 'that (one)' or 'that thing' and تو دانا is a vocative 'you, wise one'. مخوان is a negative imperative ('do not read/recite/call') in classical Persian. The clause addresses a wise person, telling him not to call or recite that thing.
segments.s772-g116: که introduces the reason/explanation clause 'for/because'. تن را is the direct object 'the body' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). پرستد is 3rd-person present of پرستیدن 'to worship/revere' ('worships'). نه here is a contrastive 'not' and راه روان = 'the way/path of the soul' (روان = soul/spirit). The clause: 'for he worships the body, not the soul's path.'
segments.s772-g117: همی is a classical continuous marker meaning 'continually/always' placed before a verb phrase. خواسته here functions as 'the wished/desired (thing)' or 'his wish', and جوید is a 3rd-person present of 'to seek' — together 'he keeps seeking what he desires'. و links to the next noun; نام بد = 'bad name' i.e. 'bad reputation'.
segments.s772-g118: بترسد is a 3rd-person present form of ترسیدن 'to fear' (here 'he fears' or 'he is afraid'). روانش = 'his soul/spirit' (possessive suffix -ش). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. فرجام بد = 'bad outcome' or 'evil end'. The clause: 'his spirit fears/dreads a bad end.'
segments.s772-g119: دگر گفت again 'another said' or 'he again said'. اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. شاه لب را ببست: لب را ببست is a past form 'shut/closed his lips' (ببست here is classical present/future or perfective 'he closes/keeps closed'); idiomatically 'keeps his lips sealed'. The clause sets the condition: 'If the king keeps his lips sealed.'
segments.s772-g120: نبیند is a negative 3rd-person present form of دیدن 'to see' (classical form meaning 'he does not see' / 'he will not see'). همی again gives continuous/emphatic sense. تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (tāj = crown/sovereignty, takht = throne). نشست here is the verb 'to sit, to be seated' or a nominalized sense 'sitting/seat' — together the object phrase means 'the crown and (the) throne's seat/enthronement' and the clause says he will not behold them. Overall: 'he will not see the crown and throne.'
segments.s772-g41: This is a noun phrase: سواران (plural noun 'horsemen/riders') is followed by two postpositive modifiers ماهوی and شوریده‌بخت. In Persian poetic word order modifiers normally follow the noun; ماهوی is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'otherworldly/ontological' and شوریده‌بخت is an epithet 'ill‑fated' describing the riders.
segments.s772-g42: بدیدند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they saw'. It governs the following noun phrase کآن خسروانی درخت, where کآن functions as a demonstrative/pointing element ('that') attached to خسروانی درخت 'royal tree'. In this poetic line the verb appears before the object phrase but the clause reads as a normal past narrative: 'they saw that royal tree.'
segments.s772-g43: This clause begins with two coordinated prepositional phrases introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from': ز تخت 'from the throne' و ز آوردگه 'and from the audience‑place/venue'. آرمید is a past tense verb 'he rested/lay at rest' and here is used euphemistically to mean he fell down or died. The sequence describes the place he fell from.
segments.s772-g44: بشد is a narrative particle/verb meaning 'it happened' or 'became' used to introduce the ensuing clause. هر کسی is an indefinite/relative 'whosoever/each person', and روی او را is the object 'his face' marked by the definite object marker را. بدید is past 'saw'. Together the clause means 'it came to pass that whoever saw his face...' (the rest of the result follows in the next line).
segments.s772-g45: گشادند here is a past plural verb 'they loosened/opened' (poetic usage) taking بند قبای بنفش as its object: بند 'the fastening/tie' of قبای بنفش 'the purple robe'. The verb preceding the object is a stylistic inversion common in poetry.
segments.s772-g46: همان is a demonstrative 'the very/that' pointing to the specific items that follow; افسر و طوق و زرینه‌کفش is a coordinated noun list 'helmet and collar and golden‑shoes'. Note زرینه‌کفش is a compound adjective+noun meaning 'golden‑shod' or 'golden shoes'.
segments.s772-g47: فگنده is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'thrown/cast down' modifying تن شاه ایران 'the body of the king of Iran'. The phrase به خاک 'to/onto the ground' completes the motion—literally 'cast down the body of the shah of Iran onto the earth.'
segments.s772-g48: پر از خون is a descriptive phrase 'full of blood' (پر ... از = 'full of'), and پهلو به شمشیر چاک describes the wound: پهلو 'the flank/side' with به used instrumentally/passively 'by/with' شمشیر 'the sword', and چاک 'gash/torn' as the resulting state. Together: 'full of blood, and his flank gashed by the sword.'
segments.s772-g81: بپرورد is a classical/poetic volitive/jussive form (3rd-person singular) with the prefix ب- expressing a wish or prayer ('may he cause/bring about'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'that'). بر تنش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his body' (بر = on/upon, تن + -ش = his body). بد رسد uses رسد (3rd-person singular) in the sense 'to happen / befall', so بد رسد = 'evil/ill befalls' (the verb is in the same volitive/optative sequence: 'may ill befall').
segments.s772-g82: این بهر ماهوی is a short noun group meaning 'for/of this nature' (بهر = 'for', ماهوی = 'of a nature/essential/character'). از introduces the clause with a causal/relational sense ('because of / with regard to this'); نفرین سزد is an impersonal present construction: سزد = 'it befits / is fitting', so 'a curse is fitting' (نفرین = 'a curse').
segments.s772-g83: دریغ is an exclamatory interjection 'alas' or 'what a pity'. آن is a demonstrative 'that/the'. سر و تاج و بالای تو is a coordinated noun phrase ('head and crown and your highness / high station'); بالای تو literally 'the height/eminence of you' = 'your high station'. The sequence is a terse poetic listing introduced by the exclamation.
segments.s772-g84: Again دریغ as 'alas'; آن = 'that'. دل و دانش و رای تو is another coordinated noun phrase: دل = 'heart' (inner feelings/mind), دانش = 'knowledge/learning', and رای تو = 'your counsel/opinion' (رای here = counsel/plan). The phrase laments these qualities in the same concise, parallel poetic form.
segments.s772-g85: دریغ = 'alas'. آن = 'that'. سر تخمه اردشیر is a noun phrase where تخمه اردشیر is a genitival compound 'the seed / offspring of Ardashir' (تخمه = offspring/lineage), so the whole means 'that head, a scion of Ardashir' or 'the head of Ardashir's line'.
segments.s772-g86: دریغ = 'alas'. این = 'this'. جوان و سوار هژیر is a coordinated phrase 'this youth and the rider/horseman Hojir' (سوار = rider/horseman, here in apposition to the name هژیر/Hojir). The clause again uses terse poetic coordination to mourn them.
segments.s772-g87: تنومند بودی is 'you were robust/strong' (تنومند = sturdy/robust; بودی = 2nd-person singular past of بودن 'to be'). خرد با روان is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'with wisdom and spirit' (خرد = wisdom, با = with, روان = soul/spirit), describing the person's intellectual and spiritual qualities in parallel to physical strength.
segments.s772-g88: ببردی is a past (2nd-person) transitive verb 'you carried / you brought' (classical form). خبر = 'news'. زین here functions as a short form meaning 'of this / from this' in the phrase 'خبر زین' (note: the provided lemma senses list for زین gave only 'saddle', so its pronominal/relational use is not among those senses and is therefore marked below as not matched). به = 'to/for', and نوشین‌روان is a compound epithet 'sweet‑souled' (the addressee/recipient). The whole means 'you brought the news of this to the sweet‑souled one.'
segments.s772-g57: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning ‘he said/commanded’ and heads the clause. کو را here is an archaic/interrogative/relative use meaning ‘who/whomever’ with the object marker را attached; به هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase (‘at the time of sleep’) introduced by به + noun (هنگام) and positions the circumstance before the verb phrase it modifies.
segments.s772-g58: از آن آسیا is a source phrase: از + آن + آسیا = ‘from that mill’ (آسیا = mill, not the continent). افگنند is an older 3rd-person plural verb form ‘they cast/they threw’; اندر آب (‘into the water’) marks the destination using اندر (= in/into) plus آب. The clause completes the order expressed in the previous segment.
segments.s772-g59: بشد is a past/copular verb meaning ‘became’ (it became / they became) linking to the following predicate. تیز is an adjective/adverb ‘swift/quick’, and بدمهر qualifies the following noun as ‘ill-disposed/unkind’; together تیز بدمهر modify دو پیشکار (‘the two attendants’). Word order places the verb before the predicate, a common classical/poetic arrangement.
segments.s772-g60: کشیدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb ‘they pulled/dragged’. The object follows: پرخون is a preposed adjective meaning ‘blood-soaked/bloodied’, تن is ‘body’, and شهریار is the possessor/title ‘the king’. Together they form ‘they dragged the king’s bloodied body.’
segments.s772-g61: کجا here functions adverbially with the negative نشناختند to mean ‘(in) nowhere/wherever’ or ‘they did not anywhere’; ارج آن کشته is a genitive/construct-like phrase ‘the worth/honor of that slain one’ (آن + کشته). نشناختند is past negative 3rd-person plural ‘they did not recognize/know.’
segments.s772-g62: به گرداب = ‘into/toward the whirlpool’ (به here indicating motion toward), زرق is a descriptive noun meaning ‘gilding/finery’ modifying گرداب (a poetic image: a gleaming whirlpool), اندر is an additional locative/inward marker and انداختند is the 3rd-plural past ‘they threw/cast’. The doubled locatives (به ... اندر) are a poetic way to stress the motion ‘into’.
segments.s772-g63: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. شب روز شد is a verbless/compound expression ‘night became day’ using شد (‘became’) as the copula. مردم آمد پدید is a sequence of verbs: آمد ‘came’ plus پدید ‘appeared/manifested’, so ‘people came into view/appeared’ — آمد + پدید is a common periphrasis meaning ‘appeared’.
segments.s772-g64: دو مرد گرانمایه is a noun phrase ‘two esteemed/precious men’ (گرانمایه postmodifies مرد). آنجا رسید places the action locatively: آنجا ‘there’ and رسید is past ‘arrived/reached’. Word order is typical: NP then verb phrase indicating arrival.
segments.s772-g33: ز introduces a cause or source ('from/because of') followed by two participial/adjectival words (پرورده 'reared' and سیر 'sated') that describe the group; آید is the present-tense verb 'comes' placed before the demonstrative+noun phrase (این هفت گرد), a poetic word order where the verb precedes its subject for emphasis. The whole phrase means 'as a result of being reared and sated, these seven come.'
segments.s772-g34: شود کشته is a passive construction ('will be/shall be slain'); بر here is the older poetic/prepositional use linking the predicate to the person described (marked by the following apposition بی‌گنه یزدگرد), so the line states that the named king (بی‌گنه یزدگرد) will be slain. The order (verb + participle + prepositional link + appositive name) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s772-g35: بر این‌گونه is an idiomatic phrase 'in this way / thus'; the second بر before تاجداری is the preposition 'on/upon' and تاجداری is a deverbal noun 'kingship, reigning'. بمرد is an archaic/poetic past/subjunctive form of 'to die' used here narratively to mean 'he died' (the classical text often uses forms that look like subjunctive but serve as past narrative).
segments.s772-g36: که introduces a causal clause here ('because / for that'); از لشکر او is 'from his army' (از marking source/partitive, او = 'his'); سواری is 'a horseman/rider' and نبرد in this older poetic usage functions as a verb meaning 'did not go to battle / did not ride out to fight' (though the lemma listed is the noun 'battle', the context shows verbal/negative past sense). The clause explains the cause of his dying on the throne.
segments.s772-g37: خرد نیست is a simple negation 'wisdom is not (there / present)'; با introduces the accompanying/positional phrase which follows: گرد گردان سپهر is a poetic nominal group 'the sky's circling/revolving hosts' (گرد + گردان together convey the idea of 'circling/revolving'); word order places the negated predicate first then the circumstantial phrase.
segments.s772-g38: نه ... بود is another negative past construction ('nor was ...'); پیدا بود = 'was visible / apparent'; رنج و خشمش = 'his suffering and his anger' (خشم with genitive suffix -ش); ز مهر = 'because of / from love' (ز marking cause). The whole clause says that his suffering and anger were not apparent on account of love.
segments.s772-g39: همان به که is an idiomatic expression meaning 'it is best / it is fitting that'; what follows is a negative subjunctive/optative (گیتی نبینی) 'that you not see the world' with به چشم ('with the eye') as an oblique phrase specifying the manner. The verb نبینی is present/subjunctive (classical poetic usage) functioning as a recommendation or comment.
segments.s772-g40: نداری is a nominal/verbal phrase 'you do not have / there is not' (here meaning 'you have none'); ز کردار او = 'because of / on account of his deed(s)'; مهر و خشم is the coordinate object 'love and anger'. The clause asserts that, in regard to his actions, you feel neither love nor anger.
segments.s772-g49: ز پیش = a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from the presence of' (ز 'from' + پیش 'before / in the presence of'); شهنشاه is the object/goal of that preposition. برخاستند is a third‑person plural past tense verb ('they rose / got up / departed') that follows the prepositional phrase; the subject is implicit ('they'). Word order (prepositional phrase before verb) is typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s772-g50: زبان را is a definite/direct‑object/topic phrase (زبان 'speech / tongue' + را object marker). به introduces the manner/instrument (here 'with' or 'in'), نفرین is the noun 'curse', and بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arrayed' (archaic/poetic verb bi-ārāstan). Together the clause means they 'adorned their tongues with curses' — i.e. they began to curse or spoke in curses.
segments.s772-g51: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that / such that / that'). ماهوی را has the object/topic marker را attached to what here is a proper name or nominal (Māhowi) — none of the supplied dictionary senses exactly names a person, so the proper‑name reading is treated as irregular. باد تن is a poetic collocation where باد ('wind') stands for the body's breath/life (i.e. the wind of the body = breath); همچنین 'likewise / similarly' links this action to what preceded. The clause is elliptical: the 'wind/breath' leaving Māhowi is implied.
segments.s772-g52: پر از خون is a tight adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of / from' + خون 'blood'). فگنده is a past participle/archaic poetic form of فگندن ('thrown / cast') describing the body, and به روی زمین is the locative phrase 'upon the ground' (به + روی 'upon' + زمین 'ground'). Overall: '(his) body, thrown and full of blood, upon the ground.'
segments.s772-g53: به نزدیک + ماهوی = a directional/locative phrase 'toward/near Māhowi' (به نزدیک 'to/near' + ماهوی place/name). رفتند is the past plural verb 'they went / they approached'. زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' placed after the verb (poetic word order). The clause functions as the main action: 'they quickly went near Māhowi.'
segments.s772-g54: This is a nominative/appositive noun phrase that supplies the persons/items who approached: 'ابا یاره' and 'گوهر نابسود'. In context these read as names or epithets (hence the irregularity relative to the given senses). و is the coordinating 'and'. نابسود here is an epithet meaning something like 'undiminished / imperishable' attached to گوهر ('gem / jewel'). There is no new verb in this segment; these nouns supply who 'went' in the previous clause.
segments.s772-g55: به ماهوی گفتند = 'they said to Māhowi' (به introduces the indirect object). کآن is a poetic contraction of که آن ('that that / that the'), introducing the reported content; شهریار is the noun 'the king / sovereign' (the demonstrative آن is fused to the clause). Note: several lemmas in the provided lists are nominal senses not matching the inflected verb گفتند; here گفتند is the past plural 'they said.'
segments.s772-g56: بر آمد is a compound verb 'rose up / came forth' (بر + آمد, past). ز introduces source 'from': ز آرام 'from rest/peace' and از کارزار 'and from the battlefield' (two parallel prepositional phrases joined by و). The whole phrase is the content of what was told: that the king had arisen, coming from rest and from the battle.
segments.s772-g25: به نزدیک تنگ اندر آمد به هوش is composed of a locative/prepositional phrase followed by the verbal phrase 'آمد به هوش' (came to awareness). 'به نزدیک تنگ اندر' = 'into/near the narrow (place)' (اندر = in/within, archaic/poetic placement before the verb). 'آمد' is simple past 'came' and the following 'به هوش' is an idiom meaning 'to regain consciousness / become aware'.
segments.s772-g26: چنان چون کسی راز گوید به گوش is a simile clause introduced by چنان چون ('as if/just as'). کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone'. راز گوید literally 'says (a) secret' — گوید is the (literary) 3rd-person present form used for a vivid/hypothetical action here. به گوش 'into the ear' indicates whispering/close confiding.
segments.s772-g27: یکی دشنه زد بر تهیگاه شاه is subject–verb–object order: 'یکی' ('a certain one/someone') is the subject, 'دشنه زد' is the past tense 'struck/stabbed with a dagger', and 'بر تهیگاه شاه' is the prepositional/direct target ('upon the king's hollow/abdomen') — تهیگاه is a poetic term for a hollow part of the body (chest/torso).
segments.s772-g28: رها شد به زخم اندر از شاه آه uses رها شد in a passive/intransitive sense 'was released/escaped' — here it means 'a sigh escaped'. The following prepositional phrases give source/location: 'به زخم اندر' = 'from/at the wound' (literal word order inverted for poetic effect) and 'از شاه' marks the origin/possessor 'from the king'. 'آه' is the noun/interjection 'a sigh/alas' that is the thing which escaped.
segments.s772-g29: به خاک اندر آمد سر و افسرش: 'به خاک اندر آمد' = 'came down to the earth' used intransitively to mean 'fell to the ground'. The subject that falls is 'سر و افسرش' = 'his head and his afsar' — افسر here is the poetic word for helmet, with the possessive suffix -ش 'his'.
segments.s772-g30: همان نان کشکین به پیش اندرش: 'همان' ('that/the same') modifies 'نان کشکین' ('meagre/dry bread' — کشکین is a poetic epithet meaning thin/meagre). 'به پیش اندرش' literally 'in front (به پیش) of him (اندرش)' — the verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis) and this phrase means 'lay before him' or 'was before him' with اندرش carrying the sense 'in his presence / before him'.
segments.s772-g31: اگر راه یابد کسی زین جهان is a conditional clause: اگر 'if', راه یابد 'finds/attains a way' — یابد here is a present/subjunctive-like form expressing possibility. کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone'. زین جهان = 'out of/from this world' — زین is an archaic/formal form meaning 'this' (classical usage); together they mean 'from this life/world'.
segments.s772-g32: بباشد، ندارد خرد در نهان: بباشد here is a hortative/licence-like form ('let him be' / 'so be it' / 'may he be'); ندارد خرد 'he has no wisdom' (ندارد = does not possess). در نهان 'in secret/within' modifies خرد: 'no wisdom hidden within' — the sentence states there is no inner wisdom (i.e., nothing to save one from the world's fate).
segments.s772-g89: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this clause to a preceding noun. در آسیا is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Asia' (آسیا = the continent) that sets the scene. ماه‌روی تو را is a noun phrase 'your moon‑faced one' where ماه‌روی (a compound adjective/noun) precedes the possessor تو and the definite/object marker را marks the phrase as definite/direct object; the verb is omitted here by poetic ellipsis and appears later.
segments.s772-g90: جهاندار و دیهیم‌جوی is a coordination of two epithets 'sovereign and diyyah‑seeker/avenger' that further identify the moon‑faced person; تو را again attaches the possessor 'your' plus the object marker را. The clause has no finite verb here (poetic ellipsis), so these nouns function as appositive descriptions of the direct object introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s772-g91: به here functions instrumentally 'with/by' (i.e. by means of a dagger). دشنه is the instrument 'dagger', and جگرگاه names the target 'the breast/heart'. بشکافتند is a third‑person plural past verb 'they pierced/penetrated/cleft' (classical past form), so the whole phrase reads 'they pierced (him) through the breast with a dagger.'
segments.s772-g92: برهنه is an adjective 'naked/stripped' describing the person. به آب اندر is a locative/goal phrase 'into the water' (به = toward/into, اندر strengthening 'in/into'), and انداختند is the past plural 'they threw/cast'. Word order places the adjectival description before the locative and the verb at the end, giving 'naked, they threw (him) into the water.'
segments.s772-g93: سکوبا is an obscure noun (possibly a proper name or a rare term); از آن سوگواران is a partitive/relational phrase 'of those mourners' (از = from/of, آن = those/the), and the numeral four (چهار) in the segment text marks how many are meant. The segment lists the entity and a partitive specification before the verbal phrase that follows.
segments.s772-g94: برهنه شدند is a compound inchoative/passive sense 'became naked' or 'were stripped' (برهنه adjective + شدند 'they became/were made'). اندران جویبار is a locative expression 'in/therein the stream' (اندران = therein/among them, جویبار = stream), placed after the verb to indicate where this happened.
segments.s772-g95: گشاده تن is a fixed descriptive phrase 'broad‑bodied' or 'with an open/broad body' (گشاده qualifying تن). شهریار جوان follows as a title plus epithet 'the young king'; adjectives precede the noun here and multiple epithets are placed in apposition to describe the same person.
segments.s772-g96: نبیره‌ی is a noun with an ezāfe ('‑ye') marking a genitive/attributive relation: 'the scion/heir of…'. جهاندار is the possessor 'the sovereign', and نوشین‌روان is an epithet 'sweet‑souled' attached after to qualify either the sovereign or the scion in apposition; the ezāfe links the nouns as 'the scion of the sovereign, the sweet‑souled.'
segments.s772-g145: اگر starts a conditional clause ('if'). دست (subject) is followed by the adjective بیکار (idle/inactive) and then the past/neutral verb گشت 'became'. از عنان is a prepositional phrase 'from the reins' specifying the cause or source — literally 'if the hand becomes idle from the reins' (i.e. if one lets go of the reins). Order is topical/archaic (topic + adjective + verb + PP).
segments.s772-g146: روانت is روان 'soul/spirit' with the 2nd-person suffix -ت ('your soul') and functions as the object. به چنگ اندر آرد is a verbal phrase where آرد (3sg present/praesential) 'brings/puts' takes the PP به چنگ اندر 'into the claw'. سنان (spear) is the agent, placed after the verb by poetic inversion (V ... S). The tense/aspect is present/habitual or 'will' in narrative usage.
segments.s772-g147: دگر گفت = 'he said again' (دگر here = 'again'); گفت functions as the verb 'said' (classical usage). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, followed by the compound/name نامبردار 'renowned, famed' with the adjective نو 'new' after it (postpositive adjective common in poetic/classical Persian).
segments.s772-g148: تو رفتی is a independent past clause 'you have gone / departed' (رفت#رو, past 2nd sing.). و coordinates the next clause. In کردار شد پیش رو, کردار is the subject 'deed(s)/actions', شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became / came to be', and پیش رو ('before the face', i.e. 'in front') is the predicative/adverbial phrase — so literally 'deeds became before (you)' = 'your deeds stand before you'. Word order reflects classical/poetic inversion but is transparently S V complement.
segments.s772-g149: تو را is a topical/dative construction 'for you' (تو with the object/topic marker را). در بهشت اسْت تخت literally 'in Paradise is (a) throne' — subject/predicate ordering is archaic (topic + locative + verb + predicate). این بس است is a parenthetical 'this is enough / this suffices' (بس = sufficient).
segments.s772-g150: زمین بلا is a nominal clause 'the earth (is) calamity' with بلا as predicate 'calamity/affliction'. بهر دیگر کس است = 'is for any other person' where بهر functions as an older preposition 'for/to' and دیگر کس means 'another/any other person'. The copula است is at the end as expected.
segments.s772-g151: دگر گفت = 'he said again' (as above). کآن کس 'that man / the one' is followed by a relative clause introduced by که. Inside the relative clause، او functions as the pronoun 'him' (object) and کشت is the past transitive 'killed'; چون تو ('as you') is a comparative phrase indicating manner. The whole relative clause کآن کس که او چون تو کشت modifies 'that man'.
segments.s772-g152: ببیند is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to see' used narratively as 'will see / shall see / sees'. کنون 'now' modifies the verb. روزگار درشت is the object/predicate 'rough/harsh times' (روزگار = time/fate, درشت = harsh/rough). Word order here is V + temporal + object phrase typical of classical phrasing.
segments.s772-g65: از introduces a source/partitive phrase 'from/out of'; آن is a demonstrative ('those'/'that') determining سوگواران ('mourners'); پرهیزگار is a post‑nominal adjective describing the person (classical Persian often places modifiers after the noun), so the whole phrase means 'one pious among those mourners' or 'from those pious mourners.'
segments.s772-g66: بیامد is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'came' (classical narrative past). The indefinite subject یکی follows the verb (poetic V–S order) and here means 'a certain one / one person.' بر لب جویبار is a locative phrase with بر + noun ('on/upon the lip/brink') and جویبار qualifies لب as 'the bank/edge of the stream.'
segments.s772-g67: Object phrase تن او ('his body') precedes the verb (common in classical Persian poetic narrative). برهنه is an adjective modifying تن; بدید is the past tense 'he saw'; اندر آب is a locative adjunct 'in the water.' The whole clause reads 'he saw his body naked in the water.'
segments.s772-g68: بشورید here is an archaic/poetic perfective (parallel to other -ید past forms like بدید) meaning 'washed' (i.e., washed himself or the body); though the form resembles an imperative in modern Persian spelling, in context it continues the past narrative. و آمد هم links the next action ('and he came/approached as well'), with هم adding 'also' or emphasis; اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste' describing how he came.
segments.s772-g69: چنین ('thus/so') introduces the sequence; تا + verb expresses extent 'until'; در خانه راهب is the locative phrase 'into/at the monk's house' (در + noun + modifier); رسید is the past 'he reached/arrived.' The clause means 'so he went until he reached the monk's house.'
segments.s772-g70: بدان سوگواران is an indirect object phrase 'to those mourners' (here ب + demonstrative = 'to those'); it precedes the verb bگفت ('he said/told') which takes the clause آن چه دید ('that which he saw') as its object. آن چه is a relative/nominalized clause 'what/that which.' Word order places the indirect object before the verb, then the reported content.
segments.s772-g71: که introduces reported content ('that'); شاه زمانه is a genitive phrase 'the king of the age/time' (شاه + زمانه). به غرق اندرست is an older locative‑copular construction: به + غرق ('into/with drowning/being overwhelmed') + اندرست (archaic 'is in'), so together 'is overwhelmed/engulfed by the flood.'
segments.s772-g72: برهنه is a predicate adjective 'naked' fronted for emphasis; به گرداب زرق اندرست is again the locative‑copular pattern 'is in the whirlpool of zaraq/finery'—here زرق ('finery, gilding') qualifies گرداب, producing the image 'caught in a whirlpool of gaudy finery.' اندرست is the archaic 'is in/among' copula finishing the predicate.
segments.s772-g153: سقف گفت is an introductory clause with a proper name + past verb ‘said’ (here the text uses an archaic or poetic name-form). ما بندگان تویم is a nominal sentence: ما (we) + بندگان (servants) as predicate, closed by تویم, an archaic/poetic predicate form meaning “we are of you / we are yours.” Word order is Subject – Predicate – pronominal predicate (تویم) rather than modern copula syntax.
segments.s772-g154: نیایش‌کن is a compound participle/noun meaning ‘one who prays’ or ‘worshipper’ (a deverbal agent). پاک‌جان is a vocative epithet (‘pure‑souled’) addressing the addressee; it stands next to the vocative particle role even though no separate vocative marker appears. تویم repeats the predicate-form ‘we are (yours)’, linking this descriptive phrase back to the subject ‘we’.
segments.s772-g155: که introduces a wished clause (in older/poetic Persian it often heads optative or subjunctive wishes here). این دخمه = ‘this tomb.’ پر + لاله is the adjective ‘full (پر) of tulips (لاله)’ placed before the noun باغ: the phrase literally reads ‘a garden full of tulips.’ تو باد uses باد as an optative particle ‘may it be for you,’ so the overall construction is ‘that this tomb may be for you a garden full of tulips.’
segments.s772-g156: کفن دشت is an elliptic order meaning ‘the plain as (your) shroud’ (کفن = shroud, دشت = plain); Persian often places the predicate noun before the possessor/locative in poetic formulas. و connects items in the wish: شادی و راغ تو باد again ends with باد as optative ‘may joy and longing be yours.’ راغ here functions as a noun ‘longing/desire.’
segments.s772-g157: بگفتند is the past tense plural ‘they said’ in an archaic/poetic form with a prefixed ب‑. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ تابوت برداشتند is a simple past clause: تابوت (the coffin) is object and برداشتند (they lifted/took up) is the verb in the past plural.
segments.s772-g158: ز هامون = ‘from the plain’ where ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ سوی دخمه = ‘toward the tomb’ (سوی = toward(s)). بگذاشتند is past plural ‘they placed / set (it) down,’ again with the archaic b‑ prefix; word order gives a motion-from + motion‑to sequence before the placing verb.
segments.s772-g159: بر آن خوابگه رفت is a prepositional phrase + past verb: بر ... خوابگه (here بر ... رفت = ‘went to/onto that resting‑place’) with آن pointing to the place. ناکام شاه follows as a noun phrase ‘the thwarted/unsuccessful king’ (ناکام as an epithet before شاه). The sequence is Subject omitted (understood) + motion phrase + nominal epithet labeling who moved.
segments.s772-g160: سر آمد here forms an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning ‘came upon / befell.’ آمد is the past ‘came,’ and سر in this collocation functions idiomatically (thus given sense left unspecified). بر او = ‘upon him.’ رنج و تخت و کلاه is a paratactic list (‘suffering, the throne, and the crown’) of items that ‘befell’ or ‘came upon’ him; the conjunctions و simply join the items.
segments.s772-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” بشنید is simple past, 3rd-person singular “he heard.” ماهوی بیدادگر is a noun phrase in which ماهوی functions as a proper-name/epithet (the name Mahvi) and بیدادگر is an epithet “the cruel/tyrant,” so together: “the cruel Mahvi.” The whole segment sets a temporal subordinate clause: “when the cruel Mahvi heard….”
segments.s772-g2: This clause gives the content: سخن‌ها (the words/the speeches) is the thing spoken; کجا is the interrogative adverb “where” modifying گفت. گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here used as a verb, although the provided senses list is noun-form; therefore marked -1). او را is the marked indirect object “to him” (را is the definite/object marker), and پسر is the subject/agent “the son.” Literal order: “the words where said to him the son?” → “Where did the son say those words to him?”
segments.s772-g3: چنین is an adverb/adverbial particle “thus/so.” گفت here is the past tense verb “he said” (note the provided senses were nominal; used verbally here). با + آسیابان functions as “to the miller” (با can mark the person spoken with/ to). که introduces the reported imperative/content clause that follows. خیز is a direct imperative “rise!” and begins the quoted command introduced by که.
segments.s772-g4: This is a sequence of imperatives. سواران is the object “the horsemen” (what is to be brought or mounted). ببر is an imperative “take!/bring!” addressing the miller or his men. خون دشمن is a genitive phrase “the enemy’s blood” (خون + دشمن). بریز is another imperative “pour/ spill!” — together: commands to bring the horsemen and to take and spill the enemy’s blood.
segments.s772-g5: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” بشنید is past “he heard.” از او marks the source “from him.” آسیابان is the subject “the miller” (word order here places the subject after the verb). سخن is the object “the words.” Together: “When the miller heard the words from him….”
segments.s772-g6: This is a negative, verbless/elliptical observation typical of classical Persian. نه ... نه is a paired negation: نه سر دید = “he saw no head/beginning,” از آن کار = “of that affair,” پیدا = predicate “apparent/visible/found,” نه بن = “nor (its) root/foundation.” The sense is idiomatic: he could make neither head nor tail of the matter.
segments.s772-g7: شبانگاه is an adverbial/time expression “at night/ by night.” نیران is an adjective-like word “fiery/ardent,” modifying the temporal phrase (so: “a fiery night”). خرداد ماه is the calendrical phrase “the month of Khordad.” Together they form an adverbial time phrase: “on a fiery night of the month Khordad.”
segments.s772-g8: سوی is the preposition “toward.” آسیا here is the place “the mill” (not the continent). رفت is the simple past “he went.” نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase “near the king” and can either qualify the mill (‘the mill near the king’) or the motion (‘he went, near the king’); the most natural reading is that the mill is near the king. Literal: “He went toward the mill near the king.”
